intro.kicker: Topic 20 of 22 • Chapter: Newton’s Laws of Motion • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: Unlike a massless string (where tension is uniform throughout), a massive string has a varying tension at each cross-section. When a force F is applied to a string of mass m and length L, the tension at a point distance x from the force end is Tₓ = F(L−x)/L. This is a direct consequence of Newton’s Second Law applied to the string segment beyond x. NEET tests the tension formula and the ability to identify the correct fraction.

expectedQuestions.description: Direct massive-string MCQs appear once every 2–3 years in NEET. More commonly tested implicitly in rod/rope tension problems.

timeRequired.description: Four cases from Table 4.3 — 20 min; formula derivation — 10 min; PYQ practice — 10 min.

difficulty.description: The key challenge is identifying which segment exerts tension on the other. Sign errors arise when the force is applied at the far end vs. near end.

nriUsaGap.description: US AP Physics uses massless string convention almost exclusively. Position-dependent tension in massive strings is not standard US curriculum, making this a 100% new concept for NRI students.

weightage.sectionTitle: NEET Weightage — Motion of Massive String (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Tension at distance x from force-applied end: Tₓ = F(L−x)/L.
The four standard cases from Table 4.3 correspond to different applied-force and hanging configurations.

strategySteps.title: Fix the Formula Tₓ = F(L−x)/L
strategySteps.description: This follows from applying F = ma to the string segment of length (L−x) that must be accelerated. That segment has mass m(L−x)/L, and the whole string accelerates at a = F/m. So Tₓ = [m(L−x)/L] × (F/m) = F(L−x)/L.

strategySteps.title: Study All Four Cases from Table 4.3
strategySteps.description: Case 1: uniform string on frictionless surface. Case 2: force at one end, tension varies. Case 3: two opposing forces. Case 4: string hanging vertically. Each case has a different acceleration and tension formula.

strategySteps.title: Note the Endpoints
strategySteps.description: At x = 0 (force end): T = F (maximum). At x = L (free end): T = 0. These boundary conditions serve as quick checks.

cards.title: Massive String — Complete Notes
cards.description: All four Table 4.3 configurations with FBD derivations, formula sheet, boundary condition checks, and NEET solved examples.

cards.title: Position-Dependent Tension — Core Formula
cards.coreRevision: String mass m, length L, force F applied at one end. Acceleration a = F/m. Segment beyond x has mass Mₓ = m(L−x)/L. Newton’s 2nd Law on that segment: Tₓ = Mₓ × a = m(L−x)/L × F/m.
points: Tₓ = F(L−x)/L where x is measured from the applied-force end
Tₓ decreases linearly from F (at x=0) to 0 (at x=L)
cards.example: String mass 2 kg, length 4 m, force 20 N. Tension 1 m from force end: T = 20×(4−1)/4 = 15 N.

cards.title: Vertically Hanging Massive String
cards.coreRevision: String of mass m, length L hangs from a fixed support. No applied force. Tension at height h from bottom: Tₕ = mg∕L × h (weight of string below point h supports that segment). Maximum tension at the top (h = L): T = mg.
points: Tension at the top = mg (full weight of string)
cards.example: String mass 1 kg, length 2 m. Tension at 0.5 m from bottom: T = (1×10/2)×0.5 = 2.5 N.

cards.title: Two Opposing Forces (Table 4.3 Case 3)
cards.coreRevision: Forces F₁ and F₂ applied at two ends of the string, F₂ > F₁. Net acceleration a = (F₂−F₁)/m. Tension at distance x from F₂ end: Tₓ = F₂ − m(F₂−F₁)/m × x/L = F₂ − (F₂−F₁)x/L.
points: When F₁ = F₂: a = 0, tension is uniform throughout = F₁ = F₂
This checks out: no net motion, string in static equilibrium at every cross-section
Tension varies linearly from F₂ at one end to F₁ at the other
cards.example: F₂ = 30 N, F₁ = 10 N, L = 5 m. Tension at x = 2.5 m from F₂ end: T = 30 − (20/5)×2.5 = 30 − 10 = 20 N.

curriculum.hintText: US physics almost always uses inextensible massless strings. Massive strings with position-varying tension are essentially not covered in US AP Physics 1 or Physics C.

gaps.title: Position-Dependent Tension — New Concept
gaps.description: In massless string problems, tension is the same everywhere. In massive string problems, each cross-section bears only the weight/force of the portion ahead of it. NEET tests this concept through Table 4.3.
bullets: Always isolate the segment beyond the point of interest; apply Newton’s 2nd Law to that segment.
Practice checking endpoints: at the free end, T = 0; at the applied-force end, T = F.

questions.question: A rope of mass m and length L is pulled by a constant force F on a frictionless surface. The tension at a point x from the force end is:
questions.explanation: The segment of rope beyond x (length L−x) has mass m(L−x)/L and must be accelerated at F/m. So T = m(L−x)/L × F/m = F(L−x)/L.

questions.question: A uniform rope of mass 0.5 kg and length 2 m is accelerated at 5 m/s². Find the tension at the midpoint of the rope.
A hanging massive string of mass 3 kg and length 6 m. Find the tension 2 m from the bottom.
Forces 50 N and 30 N are applied at two ends of a 5 kg string. At what point from the 50 N end is the tension 40 N?

items.title: Derive Tₓ = F(L−x)/L from first principles
items.description: Use Newton’s 2nd Law on the segment of the string beyond x. Confirm boundary conditions at x = 0 (T = F) and x = L (T = 0).

items.title: Memorise all four Table 4.3 cases
items.description: Case 1: Frictionless surface; Case 2: Force at one end; Case 3: Opposing forces; Case 4: Hanging string. Know the tension and acceleration formulas for each.

items.title: Practice midpoint tension as a check
items.description: At midpoint x = L/2: T = F/2. This is a fast NEET option to check your setup.

items.title: Extend to rods and chains
items.description: The same method works for uniform rods and chains. Isolate the segment and apply F = ma.

items.question: Why does tension decrease from the force end to the free end?
items.answer: The end where force is applied must accelerate the entire string. As you move toward the free end, you are pulling less and less mass. With less mass to accelerate, Less force (tension) is needed. At the very free end, there is no string ahead to pull, so T = 0.

items.question: What is the difference between tension in a massive vs. massless string?
items.answer: Massless string: tension is the same throughout (T is constant). Massive string: tension varies at every cross-section. T decreases from the pulled end to the free end. This is why NEET asks ‘find tension at point P’ only when the string has significant mass.

items.question: Does the massive string formula apply when the string is accelerating vertically?
items.answer: Yes, but you must include gravity. For a vertical string being pulled upward with acceleration a, the tension at height h from the bottom is T = m(a+g)h/L. For free fall (a = −g), tension everywhere = 0, which makes physical sense.

grounding_summary: Page 202 contains Table 4.3 'Motion of massive string' with four rows covering uniform surface acceleration, force-at-one-end with position-dependent tension, opposing forces, and hanging string.
The OCR table confirms T_x = F(L-x)/L for the force-at-one-end case and T = mgh/L for the hanging string case.
The page header 'Newton’s Law of Motion 189' confirms this is book page 189 (OCR file page-202).

sources.title: NCERT Physics Part I — Chapter 5: Laws of Motion
sources.why_used: NCERT discusses tension in a massive rope as an application of Newton’s 2nd Law to a string segment.

sources.title: NEET UG 2026 Physics Strategy — Massive String Applications
sources.why_used: Identifies tension in massive ropes and strings as moderate-frequency NEET topics under Laws of Motion.

review.notes: Content grounded in page 202 OCR Table 4.3 which lists all four massive-string motion scenarios. Key formula verified: T_x = F(L-x)/L.
